Title: Trump's summit delay casts pall over US-China trade truce
Article:
US President Donald Trump's request to delay the planned summit in Beijing with Chinese leader Xi Jinping casts a shadow over mutual ties that have been stable since their last meeting in October, but is unlikely to cause a major setback, analysts said.
Trump's move shows how the Iran conflict has upended his foreign policy agenda and added war to trade and Taiwan among the spectrum of issues separating the world's two biggest economies.
The delay hits pause on the retooling of bilateral relations just as both sides concluded high-stakes trade talks in Paris on Monday to lay the groundwork for Trump's Beijing trip, originally slated for the end of the month.
The negotiations followed Washington's imposition of new investigations into "unfair trade practices" aimed at rebuilding trade pressure on countries around the world - including China - after the US Supreme Court struck down Trump's global tariffs last month.
"The situation is not in jeopardy, and Beijing still wants to organise the summit, but the US-Iran conflict and the Supreme Court ruling on tariff policies have complicated these efforts," said Zhao Minghao, an international relations expert from Shanghai's elite Fudan University.
"It makes US-China interactions this year more difficult because of Trump's 'war of choice' in Iran."
Zhao added the postponement would mean there was also time for more trade actions.
"The White House says it will continue its tariff policy, but without a doubt, we could see new uncertainties in this regard that impact Beijing's calculations on dealing with the US," he said.
A source familiar with the Paris talks told Reuters before the second day of meetings that China showed openness to potential additional purchases of US agricultural goods including poultry, beef and non-soybean crops.
The two sides also discussed the flow of rare earth minerals largely controlled by China and new approaches to manage trade and investment between the countries.
State-run China Daily described the negotiations in an editorial published on Tuesday as "constructive," but warned Trump that Beijing's "openness should not be mistaken for acquiescence."
"The US side should refrain from taking any further actions that could disrupt or undermine a stable China-US economic relationship. Actions that inject uncertainty — whether tariffs, restrictive measures or unilateral investigations — do just that," read the editorial.
That sentiment would similarly apply to the setting up of the summit, according to Alfred Wu of the Lee Kuan Yew School of Public Policy.
"The fluctuations (in plans) are not ideal from China's perspective, China actually wants something more predictable," he said.
US officials have sent mixed messages about the reason for the delay. On Sunday, Trump told ​Britain's Financial Times he might postpone the meeting if China did not help unblock the Strait of Hormuz.
On Monday, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Trump may need to delay the trip due to coordinating the war effort - not because of the request that China help police the strait or because of any trade disagreements.
"China's first priority remains keeping the relationship with the United States relatively steady so it can continue focusing on self-strengthening in preparation for the longer-term strategic competition ahead," said Patricia Kim, a foreign policy fellow at the Brookings Institution.
Kim added that China understood keeping Trump satisfied was key to managing bilateral ties, and would still host Trump even though it might be wary of giving him too grand a reception as the US continued military operations against Iran, a strategic partner for Beijing.
"Trump is preoccupied with a war in Iran that has not been resolved as quickly as predicted, so he is keen to ensure that the military and economic fallout from that is contained within the next couple of weeks or so ... which means planning a successful visit to Beijing is next to impossible," said Neil Thomas, a fellow on Chinese politics at the Asia Society.
"From China's perspective, there's been a lot of concern in Beijing about the lack of preparations from the American side for the summit so Chinese policymakers wouldn't mind an extra few weeks to prepare for a more substantive visit."
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Price alignment safeguards supply, curbs leakages
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Aligning fuel prices with actual market costs is critical to ensuring supply stability, as Malaysia does not function in isolation from global markets.
This has been driven by the ongoing conflict in West Asia, which has disrupted global energy supply chains.
The international supply disruptions have, in turn, pushed crude oil prices to surge over US$100 per barrel in the global market.
Petronas Dagangan Bhd managing director and chief executive officer Azrul Osman Rani said the sharp increase has led to a significant rise in import, logistics and insurance costs borne by the industry.
He said maintaining retail prices that are not aligned with market costs could increase fiscal pressure.
"When retail prices are set far below actual market costs, the burden on government subsidies rises significantly and tends to create harmful market responses, such as excessive purchasing and smuggling. This must be firmly addressed," he said on the challenges of price-setting.
The growing subsidy burden could also affect the government's ability to allocate funds to other sectors such as education, healthcare and infrastructure development.
The Finance Ministry announced last week that the retail prices of unsubsidised RON97 and RON95 petrol were raised by 60 sen per litre respectively.
At the same time, diesel prices in Peninsular Malaysia increased by 80 sen per litre.
The new pricing took effect from March 12 until today.
MORE REALISTIC PRICING
Azrul said more realistic pricing in an uncertain global environment helps maintain domestic supply stability.
He noted that accurate market-based pricing helps safeguard the country's fiscal position while preventing market distortions that could adversely affect consumers and local businesses.
He added that these mitigation measures are in line with the cautious approach taken by the government and Petronas, which are actively implementing scenario-based planning to ensure continued supply.
On market sustainability, Azrul acknowledged that Malaysia's subsidised fuel prices, which are significantly lower than the average prices in neighbouring countries, create incentives for leakages and cross-border smuggling.
This occurs when irresponsible parties exploit the situation by purchasing subsidised fuel at much lower prices in Malaysia in large quantities and reselling it abroad for substantial profits.
"When the price gap with neighbouring countries is narrowed, the risks of excessive purchasing, market manipulation and cross-border smuggling by certain parties are also reduced," he said.
He added that price adjustments that better reflect actual market conditions should be considered promptly.
Such efforts would ensure subsidies are channelled to the right target group, curb leakages and wastage, and support domestic supply stability while better protecting Malaysia's economic interests.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HCK Capital taps Binastra for Subang Sentral revival
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: HCK Capital Group Bhd has appointed Binastra Builders Sdn Bhd as the main contractor to complete the long-stalled Subang Sentral development.
The company said the RM316.5 million contract is expected to accelerate progress, offering relief to more than 2,500 buyers who have been waiting over a decade for their units.
Binastra Builders is a wholly owned subsidiary of Binastra Corp Bhd.
Subang Sentral, formerly known as Remix 1, was stalled due to environmental and structural challenges.
"We are excited to collaborate with Binastra in bringing Subang Sentral back to life. Our focus is on quality and timely delivery for all buyers," HCK executive chairman Tan Sri Clement Hii said in a statement.
"We are committed to completing the project without additional cost to purchasers, while enhancing the surrounding area with infrastructure improvements that benefit the community."
Binastra managing director Datuk Jackson Tan Kak Seng said the company had previously acted as a white knight in rescuing Comintel from its Practice Note 17 status.
"Following this, we were involved in revitalising the Empire Residence project, where three residential developments have since obtained their certificates of completion and compliance.
"With Subang Sentral, we are proud to once again contribute our expertise to revive a long-abandoned development with our new client.
"The project carries not only commercial significance but also strong social responsibility, as more than 2,500 homebuyers have waited many years for their homes," he said.
Subang Sentral is part of HCK's property portfolio, with a total gross development value of about RM15 billion across the Klang Valley.
Total construction cost for the entire project, to be developed in four phases, is estimated at more than RM1 billion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Haj cost stays put: TH maintains RM33,300 fee despite oil surge
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Lembaga Tabung Haji (TH) expects the haj cost for the 1447 Hijrah season to remain unchanged at RM33,300, despite rising global oil prices.
Group managing director and chief executive officer Mustakim Mohamad said the cost stability is supported by existing agreements with airlines, which have already factored in potential fuel price fluctuations.
"We have engagements with two airlines, namely Malaysia Airlines and Saudi Airlines, where the framework agreement already accounts for any increase in oil prices," he said at a press conference today.
Mustakim added that the agreements, which signed several years ago, include pricing mechanisms that help cushion the impact of fuel cost volatility on flight expenses.
"As such, for the 1447 Hijrah (2026) season, we are confident that the haj cost will remain at RM33,300, as the current pricing structure has already taken into account potential increases in aviation costs.
"However, if the conflict in the Middle East continues to last a long time and affects the price of oil, the Haji's cost in the future may be reduced again. It depends on how long the situation lasts," said Mustakim.
Mustakim noted that oil prices have already crossed key thresholds outlined in the agreements, which could trigger cost adjustments.
"There is a clause in the agreement that requires adjustments when oil prices exceed certain levels.
"While some increases are unavoidable, these have already been factored into this year's cost," he added.
Despite this, he said TH remains in active discussions with the airlines to control costs and keep the haj affordable, with preparations underway for the first group of pilgrims departing on April 18.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Top Glove's Q3 net profit up 1.6pct to RM30.76mil on RM1.0bil revenue
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Top Glove Corp Bhd's third-quarter net profit grew 1.6 per cent to RM30.76 million from RM30.28 million a year ago, driven by higher sales orders across most regions.
Revenue for the quarter ended Feb 28, 2026 also rose 14 per cent to RM1 billion from RM883.65 million last year, according to a bourse filing.
The glovemaker said stronger sales orders across most regions, led by a significant surge in Europe, reflected regained market share and sustained customer confidence during the quarter.
It added that higher utilisation of 89 per cent further supported cost efficiency and productivity gains, enhancing competitiveness even as average selling prices softened in line with declining raw material prices.
The company's net profit for the first half of financial year 2026 surged about 94 per cent to RM69.34 million from RM35.76 million, while the revenue rising about 6.8 per cent to RM1.89 billion from RM1.77 billion.
In a separate statement, Top Glove managing director Lim Cheong Guan said that although foreign exchange pressures weighed on performance, the dedicated efforts of its team in ensuring quality, cost efficiency and service excellence helped significantly offset the impact.
"The global environment remains fluid, including ongoing geopolitical developments, and we will continue to manage these external factors prudently and maintain stable operations," he said.
Top Glove said the Middle East conflict has given rise to global crude oil supply disruption, which affects the availability of nitrile latex, a key raw material for nitrile gloves.
The company added that it is closely monitoring these geopolitical developments and working with suppliers to ensure a stable nitrile latex supply and mitigate potential impacts.
In tandem, the group is engaging proactively with customers to keep them updated, while also encouraging the purchase of natural rubber gloves where appropriate.
Top Glove noted that its ability to switch production lines between nitrile and natural rubber gloves offers a distinct competitive advantage in managing raw material supply volatility.
Meanwhile, the group's diversified supplier base, alternative logistics routes and inventory buffers continue to minimise disruption to customer orders.
Executive chairman Tan Sri Dr Lim Wee Chai said that while the ongoing geopolitical situation, including the Middle East conflict, has created significant market, supply chain, and logistics uncertainties, these remain external factors beyond the company's control.
"What we can control and what we will continue to focus on, are our internal strengths: our quality, cost efficiency and service delivery.
"As long as we remain disciplined in these areas, vigilant and united, not only will we be able to manage the challenges ahead, but seize the opportunities they present," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Motorists urged to prioritise safety during festive exodus
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: As Malaysians prepared for the annual festive exodus, drivers are urged to plan their journeys carefully and exercise patience to ensure the safety of themselves, their passengers and other road users, the Road Safety Expert Association (RSEA) said.
Its founder Jamil Manan Supri said good trip planning was essential during peak travel periods.
"Although travelling during peak hours can be challenging, careful planning can help drivers reduce stress and lower the risk of accidents.
"Patience while driving is also crucial for road safety. Drivers should avoid aggressive behaviour such as sudden lane changes or dangerous manoeuvres, which can lead to accidents and cause traffic congestion.
"In addition, vehicles must be in good condition, and drivers should ensure they are physically fit before embarking on long journeys," he said in a statement.
Jamil said fatigue and drowsiness were among the main contributors to road accidents, especially during festive seasons.
"In the rush to visit loved ones or return to their hometowns, some drivers drove without adequate rest simply to avoid traffic. This reduced awareness of the road environment and increased the risk of accidents.
"Drivers covering long distances are advised to stop and rest at least every two hours at Rest and Service Areas (R&R) or other safe stopping points to prevent fatigue and microsleep."
He also outlined several key aspects drivers should consider before starting their journey:
Vehicle maintenance:
Check tyres, brakes, engine oil, radiator water, lights, wipers and battery to ensure the vehicle was in good condition.
Reciting a prayer before travelling:
For Muslims, reciting a prayer for protection was encouraged. Many non-Muslims also practised similar traditions.
Personal and passenger safety:
All passengers should wear seat belts, including those in the back. For young children, the use of a child safety seat was strongly recommended.
Managing vehicle breakdowns:
If a vehicle broke down, remain calm, move to the road shoulder or a safe area, switch on hazard lights and place a warning triangle at a safe distance. Passengers should also move away from traffic.
Preparation of basic vehicle equipment:
Essential items included a tyre wrench, spare tyre, warning triangle, torchlight and battery jumper cables.
Disciplined driving and obeying road laws:
Drivers must follow speed limits and avoid dangerous behaviour.
Safe following distance:
Maintain a safe distance between vehicles to prevent collisions from sudden braking.
Stopping at safe locations:
If rest was needed, use safe locations such as R&R areas or designated stopping points.
Jamil said drivers' behaviour played a key role in road safety and traffic flow during the festive season.
"The attitude of road users significantly affected both safety and traffic. For example, drivers who travelled slowly in the overtaking lane, known as lane hoggers, inconvenienced others, increased accident risks and contributed to congestion.
"Similarly, cutting queues, aggressive lane changes or showing little tolerance towards other drivers should be avoided. Drivers must also stay alert to weather conditions and the surrounding environment.
"While we cannot control the weather, we can plan our journeys more carefully if poor conditions are expected."
He added that road safety during festive seasons depended not only on law enforcement but also on the responsibility and attitude of every road user.
"Festive seasons are a time to reunite with loved ones, but the most important thing is to ensure the journey is safe. Plan your trip well, drive responsibly and always prioritise safety."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Beware litterbugs, SWCorp goes undercover on LTU expressway
Article:
KUANTAN: Plainclothes enforcement officers have been deployed along certain stretches of the Lingkaran Tengah Utama (LTU) Expressway to nab litterbugs during the upcoming Aidilfitri holiday.
Solid Waste and Public Cleansing Management Corporation (SWCorp) chief executive officer Khalid Mohamed said that by blending in with regular traffic, these officers would monitor road-users along the toll-free dual carriageway, which has been singled out as a littering hotspot.
"Over the years, we have adopted various measures to nab offenders, and the most efficient method is assigning plainclothes enforcement officers. They could be anywhere, travelling in a car or waiting in a parked vehicle.
"We can rely on closed-circuit television (CCTV) cameras, but it is difficult to track down offenders unless we manage to capture their vehicle registration numbers," he told reporters when met outside the Kuantan court complex here today.
Khalid had earlier followed proceedings in the Sessions Court, where three men were charged for discarding cigarette butts in public places.
Khalid also said deploying undercover officers to catch offenders red-handed seemed to be the most effective method, following the government's adoption of a zero-tolerance approach towards littering since Jan 1 this year.
"Enforcement officers are going undercover to maintain cleanliness in public areas, including highways and Rest and Service Areas. It also helps instil a greater sense of responsibility among the public to avoid disposing of rubbish from their vehicles.
"We will also conduct monitoring through other methods, including physical patrols at hotspot areas, CCTV surveillance, and routine rounds along certain stretches," he said.
The 229km LTU has been making headlines for the wrong reasons, as motorists have been leaving rubbish along the highway during the holidays.
The stretch between Padang Tengku and Merapoh in Lipis has been identified as a hotspot, prompting authorities to place bins at
known littering areas
.
Last week, Pahang Local Government, Housing, Environment and Green Technology Committee chairman Datuk Mohammad Fakhruddin Mohd Arif urged SWCorp and the local authorities to
step up enforcement
along the LTU.
As there are no rest stops or petrol stations along the highway, the Pahang government built four
temporary rest areas
between Bentong and Kampung Relong in Lipis last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Syndicate uses cloned identities, alters chassis numbers to mask stolen vehicles
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A vehicle theft syndicate recently busted by police employed sophisticated methods, including cloning identities and tampering with key vehicle identifiers, to evade detection.
Bukit Aman Criminal Investigation Department (CID) director Datuk M. Kumar said forensic examinations conducted on seized vehicles revealed extensive modifications aimed at concealing their true identities.
"Checks on eight of the vehicles found that all had been tampered with, involving alterations to chassis and engine numbers," he said at a press conference at Pulapol here.
Also present were Bukit Aman Criminal Investigation Department (CID) deputy director (intelligence and operations) Datuk Mohd Sukri Kaman and Criminal Investigation Department (CID) D4 division principal assistant director Senior Assistant Commissioner Noor Dellhan Yahaya.
He said investigators were able to recover two original chassis numbers through forensic processes.
"One of the recovered chassis numbers was matched to a vehicle reported stolen in Genting Highlands," he said.
Kumar said the syndicate relied on multiple layers of deception to sustain its operations.
Among the methods identified was the use of stolen vehicles or those classified as beyond economic repair (BER) as a base for cloning identities.
"These vehicles were used to create a legitimate profile, which was then transferred onto other stolen units," he said.
He said the syndicate also manipulated vehicle registration data to further disguise the origin of the vehicles.
"In some cases, new registration numbers were issued to avoid detection by enforcement authorities," he added.
Kumar said such tactics made it difficult for buyers and even authorities to immediately identify cloned vehicles without detailed forensic checks.
He added that investigations were ongoing to determine whether there were insider elements or document forgeries involved in facilitating the syndicate's activities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #GrabBersamaMalaysia provides community support during Ramadan
Article:
GRAB Malaysia is reinforcing its connection with communities across the country this Ramadan through a series of initiatives under the #GrabBersamaMalaysia campaign.
The effort brings together employees, driver- and delivery-partners, merchant-partners and residents to restore community spaces, share meals, and support families, reflecting the values of patience and gratitude.
SURAU RESTORATION IN HULU LANGAT
Grab Malaysia joined forces with GXBank, Jaya Grocer and Lembaga Zakat Selangor (LZS) to restore Surau Nur Ramadhan in Hulu Langat, a vital community space in Kampung Paya Seberang that had fallen into disrepair.
Employees, driver- and delivery-partners as well as local villagers exhibited the
gotong-royong
spirit as they repaired, repainted and refreshed the facility to serve the community during Ramadan and beyond.
"We weren't just fixing walls or applying a fresh coat of paint," said Grab Malaysia's director of country operations and mobility, Rashid Shukor.
"This surau is a place where the community gathers during Ramadan and throughout the year. Seeing our driver-, delivery-partners, employees and villagers come together to restore it while fasting reflects what #GrabBersamaMalaysia is about: a community looking after one another," he added.
The surau restoration is one part of a broader initiative spanning 16 locations nationwide.
Together with driver- and delivery-partners, Grab prepared and distributed nearly 10,000 packets of
bubur lambuk
, bringing the tradition of sharing a warm iftar meal to communities from Kota Baru to Sandakan.
IFTAR GATHERINGS FOR FAMILIES
Recognising that a partner's strength comes from their home, Grab also hosted special iftar gatherings for driver- and delivery-partners and their families in the Klang Valley.
In collaboration with LZS, children received duit raya, while families were provided with daily essentials from Jaya Grocer. The initiative acknowledges the sacrifices of the families who support Malaysia's digital economy every day.
Watch the full campaign recap on
Grab Malaysia's Instagram
.
CELEBRATING PATIENCE AND KINDNESS
The spirit of patience and kindness during Ramadan is also highlighted in Grab Malaysia's brand film,
Sabar's on the Way
.
Inspired by a real-life viral interaction between a delivery-partner named Sabar and a customer, the comedic musical reimagines the classic
Tunggu Sekejap
made famous by P. Ramlee.
The story follows Sabar navigating unexpected challenges to complete a delivery, serving as a lighthearted reminder that a little patience and kindness can go a long way.
The film also celebrates the dedication of Grab's driver- and delivery-partners and is now available on
Grab Malaysia's official YouTube channel
.
Reflecting on the initiatives, Rashid added, "Our mission has always been to grow together with Malaysia. Whether it is restoring a community space or supporting our partners and their families, these efforts reflect our belief that progress should benefit the communities we serve."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US military says hit Iran missile sites near Strait with 'bunker buster' bombs
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US military said Tuesday it had hit Iranian missile sites near the strategic Strait of Hormuz with some of the most powerful bombs in the US arsenal.
"US forces successfully employed multiple 5,000-pound deep penetrator munitions on hardened Iranian missile sites along Iran's coastline near the Strait of Hormuz," Central Command said in a statement on X.
"The Iranian anti-ship cruise missiles in these sites posed a risk to international shipping in the strait."
The bombing raid comes after Iran closed off the strategic waterway, through which one-fifth of the world's oil flows, in retaliation for the war on the country by the United States and Israel.
The bombs – which according to an Air Force Times report in 2022 cost an estimated US$288,000 each – are still less powerful than the 30,000-pound (13,600-kg) bombs dropped by the United States against Iranian nuclear sites last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran strikes Tel Aviv with cluster warheads in retaliation for killing of security chief
Article:
DUBAI/TEL AVIV: Iran targeted Tel Aviv with missiles carrying cluster warheads in what it said was retaliation for Israel's assassination of Iran's security chief Ali Larijani, Iranian state television reported on Wednesday.
The overnight attack on Tuesday killed two people in a neighbourhood close to densely populated Tel Aviv, where there are also key military facilities, bringing the death toll in Israel from the war to at least 14.
A statement by Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said weapons used included Khorramshahr 4 and Qadr missiles, both with multi-warheads.
Israel has said that Iran has repeatedly used cluster warheads, which disperse into multiple smaller explosives mid-air and spread over a wide area, making them difficult to intercept.
In Iran, a projectile hit an area near the Bushehr nuclear power plant on Tuesday evening but caused no damage or injuries, the country told the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). IAEA chief Rafael Grossi reiterated his call for maximum restraint during the conflict to avoid the risk of a nuclear accident.
Israel and the US have said preventing Iran from developing a nuclear weapons programme was one of the goals of the attacks they launched more than two weeks ago, which killed the country's supreme leader and many other top officials.
The Iranian government on Tuesday confirmed the killing of Larijani, the most senior figure targeted since the US-Israeli war's first day, when an Israeli strike killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Iran's Supreme National Security Council, which Larijani led as secretary, said Larijani's son and his deputy, Alireza Bayat, were also killed in an Israeli attack on Monday night. The targeted killings took place as the US-Israeli war on Iran shows no signs of de-escalation.
Iran's new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, has rejected proposals conveyed to Iran's Foreign Ministry for "reducing tensions or ceasefire with the United States," according to a senior Iranian official.
Khamenei, attending his first foreign-policy meeting since his appointment, said it was not "the right time for peace until the United States and Israel are brought to their knees, accept defeat, and pay compensation," according to the official.
The official did not clarify whether the younger Khamenei, who has not yet appeared in photos or on TV since being named last week to replace his slain father, had attended the meeting in person or remotely.
Iran executed a man convicted of spying for Israel, the Iranian judiciary's media outlet Mizan reported on Wednesday. The man, identified as Kurosh Keyvani, had been convicted of providing Israel's spy agency Mossad with pictures and information about sensitive locations in Iran.
Israeli strikes across Lebanon
In Lebanon, an Israeli airstrike hit Beirut's Bachoura neighbourhood in the centre of the city on Wednesday, a Reuters witness said. Loud explosions were heard in the area after the Israeli military warned residents to evacuate a building ahead of the strike.
[[nid:1399577]]
The attack was part of a broader wave of Israeli strikes on Lebanon on Wednesday, including raids on other parts of the Lebanese capital, as well as southern and eastern areas of the country, signalling an intensification of Israel's campaign against the Iran-backed Hizbollah.
Israeli strikes on Beirut early on Wednesday killed at least six people and wounded 24 others, Lebanon's health ministry said. In southern and eastern Lebanon, at least 14 people were killed in separate Israeli airstrikes, the state news agency reported, citing the health ministry.
The latest strikes pull Lebanon deeper into the war in the Middle East after Hizbollah attacked Israel on March 2, saying it aimed to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader. Israel has responded with an offensive that has killed more than 900 people in Lebanon and forced more than 800,000 from their homes, according to the Lebanese Health Ministry.
US-based Iran human rights group HRANA said on Monday that an estimated 3,000-plus people have been killed in Iran since the US-Israeli attacks began at the end of February. Iranian attacks have killed people in Iraq, across the Gulf states, and in Israel.
US targets Iran coastline near Strait of Hormuz
The United States military said on Tuesday it had targeted sites along Iran's coastline near the Strait of Hormuz with powerful "bunker buster" bombs because Iranian anti-ship missiles posed a risk to international shipping.
The strait, a transit point for a fifth of the global oil trade, remains largely closed as Iran threatens to attack tankers linked to the US and Israel. Oil prices have soared.
US President Donald Trump has repeatedly criticised allied countries in recent days for their cool response to his requests for military help to restore the passage of oil tankers through the waterway. Most US allies in the North Atlantic Treaty Organization have told Trump they do not want to get involved in the conflict, he said on Tuesday, describing their position as "a very foolish mistake."
The US has given shifting rationales for joining Israel to attack Iran and struggled to explain the legal basis for starting a new war, underscored by the Tuesday resignation of the head of the US National Counterterrorism Center, Joseph Kent. Kent wrote in his resignation letter to Trump that Iran "posed no imminent threat to our nation."
Iran has responded to the Israeli-US attacks with wide-ranging strikes on its Gulf neighbours.
Gulf Arab states have faced more than 2,000 missile and drone attacks on US diplomatic missions and military bases, as well as oil infrastructure, ports, airports, ships, and residential and commercial buildings, most of them aimed at the United Arab Emirates.
Saudi Arabia will host a consultative meeting of foreign ministers from a number of Arab and Islamic countries in Riyadh on Wednesday evening to discuss ways to support regional security and stability, the kingdom's foreign ministry said.
The International Energy Agency has said the war in the Middle East has caused the worst oil crisis since the 1970s.
Oil prices fell more than US$2 per barrel on Wednesday to pare back some of Tuesday's sharp gains after the Iraqi government and Kurdish authorities reached a deal to resume oil exports via Turkiye's Ceyhan port, providing modest relief to concerns about supplies.
But with no signs of a de-escalation in fighting, Brent futures prices have settled above US$100 per barrel for the prior four consecutive sessions.
Oil prices are up around 45 per cent since the start of the war on Feb 28, raising concerns of a renewed spike in global inflation. The World Food Programme said tens of millions of people will face acute hunger if the war continues through June.
Global airlines sounded the alarm on Tuesday over soaring jet fuel prices, warning of hundreds of millions of extra costs, higher fares, and cuts to some routes. Global aviation has been thrown into turmoil, with flights cancelled, rescheduled, or rerouted as most Middle East airspace remains closed amid fears of missile and drone attacks.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RTD deploys 245 officers to monitor accident-prone areas during Hari Raya
Article:
BATU KAWAN: The Road Transport Department (RTD) in Penang has deployed 245 officers to monitor 18 accident-prone locations during the Hari Raya Aidilfitri 2026 operation, which runs from March 13 to March 29.
The move aimed to reduce road accidents in the state by 10 per cent during the festive period.
Penang RTD director Zulkifly Ismail said enforcement is conducted for 16 hours daily, and 24 hours during full movement restrictions across five districts — Seberang Perai Utara (SPU), Seberang Perai Tengah (SPT), Seberang Perai Selatan (SPS), Timur Laut and Barat Daya.
"We identified eight frequent accident areas on the mainland, namely Jalan Teluk Air Tawar to Jalan Bagan Luar, the North-South Expressway in SPU, Jalan Perusahaan Perai in SPT, and Jalan Besar Simpang Ampat in SPS.
"On the island, the locations included the Tun Dr Lim Chong Eu Expressway, Jalan Paya Terubong in Timur Laut, as well as Jalan Tun Dr Awang and Jalan Sultan Azlan Shah in Barat Daya," he said.
"The main focus of this year's operation was to reduce the accident rate by 10 per cent compared with 2025," he said at the Road Safety Advocacy and Sahur Stopover programme at the Bukit Tambun toll plaza northbound last night.
During checks yesterday, authorities found families crammed into vehicles, with mothers holding young children, despite the mandatory use of child restraint seats (CRS) since Jan 1, 2020.
Zulkifly said while summonses could be issued, the operations focused on advocacy to raise road user awareness, targeting a 75 per cent compliance rate of enforcement (POBC) during the festive period.
He also said that some lorry drivers continued to flout movement restrictions, which were only in effect for four days before and after Hari Raya — March 19 to 20 and March 28 to 29.
"Some claim the goods are urgently needed. Advance notices were given for delivery scheduling, and lorries caught operating during the ban would be seized," he said.
RTD also conducted bus inspections at depots three days before Hari Raya, issuing inspection slips to ensure all public transport vehicles were roadworthy and safe.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Taxpayers need not amend 2025 returns over employer tax marking errors
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Individual taxpayers are not required to file an amended Income Tax Return Form (BNCP) for the 2025 Year of Assessment if they made an inadvertent error in the 'Tax Borne by Employer' section.
The Inland Revenue Board (IRB), in a statement today, said it has reviewed the cases and identified those involved.
For cases confirmed as marking errors, excess tax refunds will be processed in stages.
"The IRB noted that some taxpayers mistakenly marked the 'Tax Borne by Employer' section when completing their Income Tax Return Forms for the 2025 Year of Assessment, resulting in delays in receiving excess tax refunds.
"For confirmed marking errors, refunds will be processed in stages. Accordingly, taxpayers do not need to submit an amended 2025 BNCP to correct this," it said.
The IRB said its tax refund system operates automatically based on the information provided in the BNCP.
"Therefore, the accuracy of the information submitted is crucial to ensure the refund process runs smoothly without delays."
The board said it remains committed to managing tax refunds fairly, transparently and with integrity, in line with efforts to improve the efficiency of the country's tax administration.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australia's Rest to invest up to US$250mil in US retail property fund
Article:
SYDNEY: Australian pension fund Rest said on Wednesday it would invest up to US$250 million in a US retail property fund, as it banks on traditional retailing remaining strong across America.
The investment will be made to the US Cities Retail Fund (USCRF), managed by Nuveen Real Estate, which owns neighbourhood shopping centres across the country's major metropolitan areas.
The fund currently holds 10 retail properties in cities including Austin, Philadelphia and San Diego, with five additional shopping centres recently secured for inclusion in the portfolio. Rest said the centres were tenanted by major US grocery stores and retailers such as Trader Joe's, The Fresh Market and Harris Teeter.
"We like the fact that if you think of a household budget, this is, along with housing, the first dollar (spent) out of that budget," Andrew Bambrook, Rest's head of real assets – investments, told Reuters in an interview.
"We've seen these sort of assets perform really well in both up cycles, but also in more challenged market cycles.
"There's not the same level of fluctuation that you might get with some of that more discretionary retail."
US consumer spending, which accounts for more than two-thirds of economic activity, increased 0.4 per cent in January after advancing by the same margin in December, figures published last week showed. Despite the lift, US gross domestic product growth slowed at a sharper pace than initially thought in the fourth quarter.
Bambrook said the investment was the first of its kind for Rest in the US retail sector. The fund, he said, was also exposed to future growth in online shopping with investments in warehouse and logistics projects around the world.
"Our view is that we absolutely think there's a place for both," he said.
"We do expect the bricks-and-mortar component to remain relevant, and in particular, this sort of necessity-based grocery retail that services local catchments."
Rest said the investment was expected to deliver stable, risk-adjusted returns across market cycles for its more than 2.0 million members, while strengthening diversification within its property portfolio.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mother allowed to join judicial review over daughter's death inquest
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The High Court here has allowed an application by Noraidah Lamat to be joined as an interested party in a judicial review.
A non-governmental organisation, who filed the judicial review, sought from the court to quash the inquest into Noraidah's daughter Zara Qairina Mahathir's death.
High Court judge Datuk Celestina Stuel Galid made the decision after considering written submissions from both Noraidah and Pertubuhan Kebajikan Ekonomi Semangat Bersatu Malaysia (Pembela), as well as oral clarifications from Pembela.
Noraidah was named as an interested party through her leave application filed in January 2025, while Pembela is the applicant. The Attorney-General and the Coroner's Sessions Court are the first and second respondents, respectively.
The court also ordered the applicant to serve all the relevant documents relating to the judicial review on Noraidah by tomorrow and fixed e-review on March 26.
Noraidah was represented by lawyers Shahlan Jufri and Mohd Syarulnizam Mohd Salleh, while Pembela was represented by Jul Hamri Jumhani.
The Attorney-General was represented by Senior Federal Counsel Mohammad Solehheen Mohammad Zaki.
On Nov 21, 2025, Pembela filed the judicial review seeking to quash the entire inquest on grounds that it was conducted under the federal Criminal Procedure Code instead of Sabah's Inquest Ordinance 1959.
The group argued that the state law contains specific procedural safeguards and should govern the inquiry, and is seeking orders to declare the 1959 Ordinance as the applicable law and to strike out the ongoing proceedings.
On Feb 11, a Sessions Court here stayed
contempt of court proceedings
against former Sabah chief minister Datuk Seri Panglima Mohd Shafie Apdal.
Judge Amir Shah Amir Hassan among others held that proceedings would be stayed pending disposal of a judicial review to challenge the legality of the inquest at a High Court here.
The Sessions Court then fixed March 27 to re-mention the case and update parties on the status of the judicial review.
Zara, a Form One student, was found unconscious in her hostel on July 16, 2025 and was pronounced dead the following day at Queen Elizabeth Hospital.
The inquest began on Sept 3, 2025, with 64 witnesses called to testify. Proceedings are scheduled to resume on March 31.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 1,054 litterbugs caught by SWCorp since Jan 1
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The Solid Waste and Public Cleansing Management Corporation (SWCorp) has detained 1,054 litterbugs nationwide since Jan 1.
Housing and Local Government Minister Nga Kor Ming, who announced this, said all those detained would be prosecuted.
"Many have already started serving community service sentences. Some were fined RM2,000 and ordered to sweep roads, clean drains, or scrub toilets for 12 hours.
"If it cannot be completed in one day, they must do it over six sessions of two hours each," he said after launching the national-level Tetra Pak 'Sort, Drop & Recycle The Carton' project.
Nga said that the majority of those committing minor littering offences were caught throwing cigarette butts.
"Do not let one cigarette butt lead to a RM2,000 fine and 12 hours of sweeping roads. It is not worth it."
He said the public should set a good example for foreign tourists by not littering.
"After the Hari Raya period, there will be another large-scale session to clean the surrounding streets. This is part of the public cleansing reforms we are implementing starting this year," he said.
It was previously reported that community service orders under the Solid Waste and Public Cleansing Management Act came into effect on Jan 1, 2026, as a penalty for minor littering in public places.
Meanwhile, Nga reminded motorists not to treat roads as "rubbish bins", particularly during the festive season.
"If you have rubbish, keep it in the car and dispose of it in a bin once you reach your destination. Community service sentences await you if caught.
"It does not matter who you are, adults, children, or foreigners, the punishment applies to everyone.
"More than 300 SWCorp enforcement officers will be on duty to monitor the situation during this Raya period. We need cooperation from all parties; this is not just the government's responsibility," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Driver's delay at petrol station sparks viral frustration ahead of Hari Raya [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A driver blocking motorists from refuelling at a petrol station while organising his car's boot has sparked frustration and a viral debate ahead of Hari Raya.
The incident was captured in a video posted by TikTok user @missdiejaaa__, showing her irritation at a man who remained parked for an unusually long time, causing a queue behind him.
In her caption, she wrote: "My husband just went to check if he would move. Where is the common sense?"
She added: "Help, I want to go back for Hari Raya, please have common sense! Other people are queuing to fill up."
The driver had been tidying his car's boot while the line behind him jammed.
"Others were waiting for him to move, and he had not filled up or had perhaps already finished filling up, yet he lingered there for a long time," she said.
The clip struck a chord with viewers, many of whom face similar frustrations at crowded locations during festive travel.
Netizens flooded the comments with humour, sympathy, and advice. TikTok user @Lana Jomari said: "If it's done, why stay there so long? Other people want to use it."
@Siti Zulaiha Mohammad added: "If it were me, I'd honk behind them or at least let them know the back is jammed already."
@Izzuddin Gadaffi advised: "If you want to hang around or organise your things, park outside. People like this really have no common sense."
User @nia wrote: "Eh, I'm also angry!" while @Azi commented: "Selfish type. Only cares about self, doesn't consider others."
@HunnyHisham observed: "I really get annoyed if it's like this, unless the place is not crowded, but if people are there, don't take too long."
The video has sparked wider discussion on public courtesy and patience, particularly during busy festive periods when queues are inevitable.
@missdiejaaa__
Laki aku baru nak pegi sound nasib dia gerak. Mana pergi common sense eh ?
♬ original sound  - Missdiejaaa
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Crest Builder unit secures RM513mil new jobs, boosting order book to RM2bil
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Crest Builder Holdings Bhd's wholly-owned subsidiary, Crest Builder Sdn Bhd (CBSB), has secured two Letters of Award (LoA) with a combined value of RM513 million.
The first, valued at RM246.4 million, covers construction of the East One SJCC development in Subang Jaya, Selangor, for Sime Darby Property (Subang) Sdn Bhd, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Sime Darby Property Bhd.
The project comprises 926 serviced apartment units across two 36-story towers, complete with resident facilities, a mechanical floor, and a podium car park. Construction is expected to take approximately 30 months following site possession.
The second LoA, worth RM266.5 million, is from Freedom Estates Sdn Bhd, a subsidiary of BRDB Developments Group, for the Miranda Hill development in Mont Kiara, Kuala Lumpur.
This project involves two towers of 38 and 36 stories, housing 552 condominium units along with car parks and recreational facilities. Completion is projected within 19 months from site possession.
In a statement, Crest Builder said these wins push the group's total outstanding order book to a record RM2 billion, adding that it will underpin earnings growth over the next four years.
Group managing director Eric Yong said, "We welcome the raging Year of the Fire Horse with significant momentum, clinching these two major contracts to start the year with a bang." The East One SJCC award marks our fourth contract from Sime Darby Property, following the successful delivery of the KL East Mall, Maya Ara, and Serasi projects.
"Our enduring partnership with Sime Darby Property, one of the nation's premier developers, is a testament to our commitment to quality and ability to meet the rigorous standards of industry leaders. In addition to strengthening existing ties, we are delighted to welcome BRDB Group into our prestigious client list."
Yong added that the RM513 million in new contracts gives Crest Builder an early lead in replenishing its 2026 order book and provides a solid platform to focus on project execution and operational excellence.
"With a historic RM2 billion order book in hand, we are well positioned to optimize our resources and ensure the timely delivery of all our ongoing developments," Yong said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Police warn buyers against 'too good to be true' car deals amid cloning syndicate bust
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have urged the public to exercise caution when purchasing used vehicles, warning that attractive deals could be linked to criminal syndicates involved in vehicle cloning.
Bukit Aman Criminal Investigation Department (CID) director Datuk M. Kumar said buyers should be wary of prices that were significantly lower than current market value.
"A price that is too cheap should raise suspicion about the vehicle's actual status. The public must scrutinise the sales process to avoid falling victim to fraudulent transactions.
"Transactions conducted purely in cash without proper documentation or clear transaction records should be avoided," he said at a press conference at Pulapol here.
Also present were Bukit Aman Criminal Investigation Department (CID) deputy director (intelligence and operations) Datuk Mohd Sukri Kaman and Criminal Investigation Department (CID) D4 division principal assistant director Senior Assistant Commissioner Noor Dellhan Yahaya.
Kumar added that buyers should be cautious if a vehicle could not be financed through legitimate financial institutions.
"This could indicate irregularities in the vehicle's ownership or documentation," he said.
He said cloned vehicles were often sold through used car dealer networks, private sellers and online platforms, making them harder to detect.
Kumar said that buyers should conduct thorough checks, including verifying ownership details with the Road Transport Department (RTD).
"Ensure all documents are genuine and match the vehicle's physical details before proceeding with any purchase," he said.
He warned that individuals who knowingly purchased or dealt in cloned vehicles could face legal consequences.
"Police will take firm action against anyone involved in activities related to vehicle theft and identity cloning, as these crimes pose a threat to public safety," he said.
Kumar also urged members of the public to channel any information on such activities to the police to assist ongoing investigations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Police bust vehicle cloning syndicate, seize RM1.1mil worth of cars
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have crippled a vehicle theft and cloning syndicate operating across several states with the arrest of 12 people and the seizure of vehicles worth an estimated RM1.1 million.
The arrests were made during a coordinated operation conducted by Bukit Aman's Criminal Investigation Department (CID) D4 division, together with contingent CID teams, between March 12 and March 15.
Bukit Aman Criminal Investigation Department (CID) director Datuk M. Kumar said the operation, dubbed Op Lejang Mewah Klon, was the result of nearly five months of intelligence gathering.
"Those detained comprised 10 men and two women, aged between 33 and 60, all locals believed to be directly involved in the syndicate's activities," he said.
He said investigations revealed that several of the suspects had acted as intermediaries in the buying and selling of stolen vehicles that had been cloned.
Police also detained a local man wanted by the Singapore Police Force (SPF) in connection with vehicle theft cases in the republic.
The suspect was believed to have played a role in smuggling stolen vehicles from Singapore into Malaysia.
Kumar said a total of 12 vehicles of various makes were seized during the operation.
Among the vehicles confiscated were Toyota Vellfire, Toyota Estima, Toyota Altis, Toyota Harrier, Mazda 3, Honda City, Honda City Hatchback and Toyota Camry.
"Four of the seized vehicles were believed to have been stolen in Singapore before being brought into Malaysia," he said.
Kumar said the case was being investigated under Section 379A of the Penal Code for stealing a motor vehicle or any component of a motor vehicle, which provides for imprisonment of up to seven years and may also include a fine upon conviction.
He added that those detained had been released on police bail, except for the man wanted by the Singaporean authorities.
"The suspect wanted in Singapore was handed over to the SPF for further investigation into the vehicle theft case there," he said.
Kumar added that police were now working closely with the SPF to facilitate further investigations and potential cross-border action.
"Investigations are ongoing to identify the full extent of the syndicate's network, which is believed to span multiple states," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Najib moves to reinstate RM1.9mil suit against Tommy Thomas
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Datuk Seri Najib Razak has filed a motion seeking leave to appeal to the Federal Court to reinstate his RM1.9 million civil lawsuit against former attorney-general Tan Sri Tommy Thomas.
The application, filed on Feb 10, follows a Court of Appeal decision that unanimously upheld a 2022 Kuala Lumpur High Court ruling dismissing the former prime minister's suit.
Najib, 72, is seeking to appeal based on three legal questions regarding his claim of misfeasance in public office arising from his prosecution in the 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB) case.
His lawyer, Yudistra Darma Dorai, said the leave application is scheduled for hearing on July 30.
In January, a three-member Court of Appeal bench led by Datuk Ravinthran N. Paramaguru
ruled
that the suit was premature.
The bench agreed with the High Court that because the criminal cases had not concluded when the civil action was filed, the claims for misfeasance, malicious process, and negligence could not proceed.
The bench also found that the tort of malicious process does not exist in Malaysian law.
Najib sued Thomas over the exercise of prosecutorial discretion under Article 145(3) of the Federal Constitution concerning the 1MDB and International Petroleum Investment Co (IPIC) cases.
The former Pekan member of parliament claimed Thomas decided to prosecute him on multiple graft charges despite the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission finding no wrongdoing in its investigation papers.
Najib further alleged that Thomas, who served as AG from June 4, 2018, to Feb 28, 2020, made disparaging comments against him prior to his appointment.
Claiming the prosecution damaged his reputation and political career, Najib is seeking RM1.9 million in damages and a declaration that Thomas committed misfeasance in public office.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police bust vehicle cloning syndicate, seize RM1.1mil worth of cars
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have crippled a vehicle theft and cloning syndicate operating across several states with the arrest of 12 individuals and the seizure of vehicles worth an estimated RM1.1 million.
The arrests were made during a coordinated operation conducted by Bukit Aman's Criminal Investigation Department (CID) D4 division, together with contingent CID teams, between March 12 and March 15.
CID director Datuk M. Kumar said the operation, dubbed Op Lejang Mewah Klon, was the result of nearly five months of intelligence gathering.
